title: Is It Not a Democratic Duty to Dispel Doubts?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. Visual inspection confirmed the opening date as 7-8-2015 and showed that the stray mixed-script line after The Hindu quotation was a page-transition OCR artefact, not printed prose. Clearly visible character and word-joining errors were corrected only in this translation file.
paragraphs: Following reports that Prime Minister of India Narendra Modi, who came to Chennai on 7 August 2015, went to Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu Jayalalithaa's residence to meet her and also attended a luncheon there, several newspapers published reports claiming that an alliance had been formed between the AIADMK and BJP for the 2016 Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly election, and that even the number of seats for the BJP had been discussed and settled.

In particular, Maalai Malar reported in detail: “Modi did not take any Tamil Nadu leaders with him to the meeting at Poes Garden. Modi, who spoke with Jayalalithaa for fifty minutes, discussed contesting the coming Assembly election in alliance with the AIADMK. Jayalalithaa is said to have laid down conditions, including that the AIADMK must lead the alliance and contest a majority of the seats. Modi's side is reported to have agreed. The two leaders are said to have virtually finalised the alliance and discussed and decided even the sharing of constituencies. The BJP reportedly asked for as many as sixty-five seats, but Jayalalithaa did not agree. When the DMDK was part of the AIADMK alliance, it was allotted forty seats. She is said to have agreed to allot the BJP forty seats in the same way. It is said that formal announcements about this finalised alliance and seat-sharing arrangement will be made when the election draws near.”

Tamil Nadu Congress Committee President E.V.K.S. Elangovan told reporters in Tiruchi that the meeting between Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Chief Minister Jayalalithaa had taken place with the Assembly election in mind, and that it had exposed the covert political relationship between the BJP and AIADMK. Yet neither the Prime Minister's side nor the Chief Minister's side has offered any explanation of this meeting so far. Political observers, quite naturally, raise certain doubts.

In the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa, she was convicted by the Special Court, acquitted by the Karnataka High Court on erroneous grounds, and the case is now being heard by the Supreme Court of India. In this situation, some may wonder whether it was proper for Prime Minister Narendra Modi to go to the home of the accused Jayalalithaa and attend a luncheon there.

The BJP's Tamil Nadu president may say that Modi's visit to an unwell Jayalalithaa was an act of political civility. But is it appropriate for someone who is unwell to give a feast, and for another to partake in it? Newspapers reported that an AIADMK Member of Parliament had threatened to cut out the tongue of anyone who said Jayalalithaa was unwell. Did he read what the BJP's Tamil Nadu president said? The BJP State president says she is unwell, while the Prime Minister from that very party goes to Jayalalithaa's home and eats a feast. Some may harbour doubts because of this contradiction.

When Prime Minister Modi himself goes to the home of Jayalalithaa, who stands accused in a disproportionate-assets case, and returns after attending a luncheon, some may wonder what approach the judges hearing Jayalalithaa's cases will take.

Even if the judges independently deliver a judgment founded upon justice, might not some suspect that the judgment took that form only because the Prime Minister and Chief Minister had met? When the Prime Minister and Governor attended the National Handloom Day function in Chennai, why did the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu, who had gone to the airport and waited half an hour to welcome the Prime Minister, alone fail to attend? Does the Chief Minister dislike weavers? When the Prime Minister visits a State to participate in a government function, it is customary for that State's Chief Minister to attend as well. Some may wonder whether it was right for the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister to stay away.

Moreover, at government functions attended by very important guests such as the Prime Minister, it is not customary to read out a greeting or speech from someone who is absent. Yet at this event attended by the Prime Minister, a speech by Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, whose name did not even appear on the invitation, was read aloud. Some may ask whether this was customary. Rather than handing over the memorandum of requests for Tamil Nadu's development projects at the Secretariat, or publicly when she went to welcome him at the airport, was it proper protocol for the Chief Minister to summon the Prime Minister to her own home and hand it to him there? Some may raise that doubt.

Having gone to the airport to welcome the Prime Minister, should not Chief Minister Jayalalithaa also have gone there to see him off, as convention required? That is courtesy, is it not? Some may wonder why she did not come to uphold that convention of courtesy.

At that sorrowful moment when Sasiperumal, a distinguished Gandhian who had fought for prohibition, was being buried a week after his death, all those committed to prohibition were grieving and paying tribute to him. At that very turbulent time, parties across Tamil Nadu were holding protests demanding the implementation of prohibition. Some may wonder whether it was civilised for the Chief Minister to invite the Prime Minister to a feast then, and whether it amounted to treating the loss of an important person with indifference.

The Hindu of 8 August 2015 carried the headline, “Skips protocol; calls on C.M. at her residence.” Its opening paragraph said: “Not allowing protocol to stand in the way of political calculations, Prime Minister Narendra Modi met Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa at her Poes Garden residence on Friday, and held one-to-one discussions on the prevailing political situation and pressing issues concerning the Centre and the State.” Some may point out that The Hindu itself wrote that the Prime Minister did not allow protocol to concern him, and raise doubts!

When Chief Minister Jayalalithaa invited the Prime Minister to her own residence, Poes Garden being a property implicated in the disproportionate-assets case, and handed him a memorandum containing the AIADMK Government's demands, why did she not mention the wage-revision agreement affecting workers at the Neyveli Lignite Corporation, a Central public-sector undertaking employing 22,000 workers, where a strike had continued for the preceding twenty days? Why did she not mention the continuing protests there? Some may ask this with regret.

Even in an interview yesterday, Communist Party of India General Secretary Sudhakar Reddy pointed out: “If the Prime Minister wishes to meet the Chief Minister of a State, the Chief Minister should be invited to Raj Bhavan for the meeting. But Prime Minister Modi broke convention and met the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister at her residence.” Some may have doubts on that account as well.

Prime Minister Narendra Modi has travelled to many States to participate in many events. In no State, not even in States governed by his own party, has he gone to the Chief Minister's residence and attended a feast. Some may therefore wonder whether it was proper to break convention only in Tamil Nadu.

When former President of India Abdul Kalam died, the Chief Minister could not go. She could not attend the Iftar event held at her invitation for distinguished members of the Muslim community. Why, four or five months have passed since Tamil Nadu's budget was adopted, yet this Government has been unable to convene the Assembly and pass the demands for grants. With the demand for prohibition rising throughout the State and spontaneous protests taking place, it has not come forward to convene the Legislative Assembly for a debate. In these circumstances, how is it able to host a feast for the Prime Minister alone? Some may ask that question.

Should not the Prime Minister's Office or the Chief Minister's Office dispel, through a proper explanation, all these doubts that journalists, neutral observers, and political commentators have raised and widely debated? Will they do so? Is it not a democratic duty to dispel doubts?
